---
title: "Chapter 16: Using Ratings by Trained Observers"
description: "Detailed study notes for Chapter 16 of Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation."
book_id: "handbook-practical-program-evaluation"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Chapter"
chapter_number: 16
source_pages: "454-487"
citation_key: "newcomer_hatry_wholey_2015_handbook_practical_program_evaluation"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:handbook-practical-program-evaluation:ch16-chapter-16-using-ratings-by-trained-observers:c0001"
  - "chunk:handbook-practical-program-evaluation:part-2:c0001"
source_status: "needs_review"
---

## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2015</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Chapter 16</span><span>Source pages 454-487</span></div>

This note summarises Chapter 16, "Using Ratings by Trained Observers," from Kathryn E. Newcomer,
Harry P. Hatry, and Joseph S. Wholey's *Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation*
[@newcomer_hatry_wholey_2015_handbook_practical_program_evaluation]. It is a paraphrased study note
based on source-linked MCP research-library retrieval records, not a substitute for the chapter.

The retrieval trail is retained in the source records at the bottom of the page. The note paraphrases the chapter's argument and procedures, and it does not reproduce textbook tables, checklists, figures, boxes, or extended passages.

## Core Argument

Trained observer ratings turn observed conditions into systematic data when definitions, rating anchors, sampling, training, and supervision are explicit.

## Study Summary

The chapter explains how anchored ratings can assess physical conditions, service settings,
functioning, or maintenance quality.

It covers deciding what to observe, defining rating scales, preparing forms, training raters,
pretesting, sampling, and collating data.

The main risk is producing subjective labels that cannot be verified, compared, or acted on.

Within the wider book, this chapter belongs to Part 2: Practical Data Collection Procedures. It
should be read alongside the neighboring chapters because the handbook treats design, data
collection, analysis, and use as connected evaluation work.

## Key Concepts

- **trained observer rating**: Structured observation by trained raters using defined and anchored rating scales.
- **data quality**: The completeness, accuracy, consistency, accessibility, and interpretability of data for evaluation purposes.
- **fieldwork**: Systematic in-person inquiry using interviews, observations, documents, and site-level evidence.

## Chapter Structure

- Design problem: Trained observer ratings turn observed conditions into systematic data when definitions, rating anchors, sampling, training, and supervision are explicit.
- Evidence focus: The chapter explains how anchored ratings can assess physical conditions, service settings, functioning, or maintenance quality.
- Practice method: It covers deciding what to observe, defining rating scales, preparing forms, training raters, pretesting, sampling, and collating data.
- Quality risk: The main risk is producing subjective labels that cannot be verified, compared, or acted on.

## Practical Implications

- Define every rating category concretely.
- Use training and trial runs before data collection.
- Plan how observations will be stored, checked, and reported.

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Reading route: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Concepts synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)
- Previous: [Chapter 15: Role Playing](chapter-15-role-playing.qmd)
- Next: [Chapter 17: Collecting Data in the Field](chapter-17-collecting-data-in-the-field.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [Evaluation Planning and Design](section-01-planning-and-design.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [Planning and Designing Useful Evaluations](section-02-planning-useful-evaluations.qmd)
- Legacy extract retained: [Performance Measurement and Program Evaluation](section-03-performance-measurement.qmd)

## Study Prompts

- What decision or design problem does Chapter 16 help resolve?
- Which evidence source should be reopened before using this summary in applied work?
- Which quality risk in the chapter is most likely to affect a live evaluation?

## Source Records

- `chunk:handbook-practical-program-evaluation:ch16-chapter-16-using-ratings-by-trained-observers:c0001`
- `chunk:handbook-practical-program-evaluation:part-2:c0001`

## References
